book_claims.claim: Superior source of antioxidants due to generous amounts of alpha-carotene, zeaxanthin and lutein
book_claims.mechanism: Multiple carotenoid types provide broader antioxidant coverage than beta-carotene alone

supplementary_research.finding: Higher serum alpha-carotene concentrations inversely associated with risk of death from all causes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer in a large NHANES study (n=15,318). Pumpkin is one of the richest dietary sources of alpha-carotene.

Pumpkin seed oil contains high concentrations of delta-7-sterols which may inhibit DHT production. Pumpkin flesh carotenoid content varies 2-10x by cultivar, with deep orange varieties having the most.
Pumpkin polysaccharides demonstrate immunomodulatory, anti-tumor, and hypoglycemic effects in animal models. The multi-carotenoid profile provides broader protection than any single carotenoid.

culinary_pairings.source: Fat essential for carotenoid absorption; roasted pumpkin with EVOO is a staple
Classic Italian pairing: pumpkin ravioli with brown butter and sage
Pumpkin hummus; complementary textures and nutrients in tagines
Warm spice amplifies pumpkin's natural sweetness; anti-inflammatory synergy
Pumpkin curry with coconut milk; fat carrier for carotenoid absorption
Classic warm spice pairing in pumpkin soups and baked goods

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: One of the richest sources. Associated with reduced all-cause mortality. Fat co-ingestion increases absorption significantly.
Converted to vitamin A as needed. Cooking increases bioavailability by breaking cell walls.
Protect retinal macula from oxidative damage. Fat-soluble; pair with olive oil.
Highly bioavailable. Important for blood pressure regulation.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 0.5 g (flesh) but pumpkin seeds contain 18 g
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Flesh is low-fiber but very nutrient-dense. Seeds are the fiber powerhouse.
